attributes.stem:
A 65-year-old man presents to his General Practitioner for an annual health check. His medical history includes hypertension, type 2 diabetes mellitus, and gout, all of which are well-controlled with medication. He reports compliance with his medications. He has recently noticed discomfort in his lower limbs, particularly towards the end of the day. Additionally, he mentions vague symptoms such as mild swelling in the lower legs, a feeling of heaviness, occasional fatigue, generalised aches and pains, subtle shifts in mood, and occasional digestive discomfort. He also mentions spending considerable time gardening in recent weeks.

On examination, his BMI is 34, and his blood pressure is 146/86. All other vital signs are normal. Systemic examination is unremarkable. There are no signs of active infection. There is localised, pitting oedema. There is no tenderness on palpation over the affected areas, and there is no appreciable warmth. Peripheral pulses are palpable bilaterally, with no significant discrepancies between limbs. (Image).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with lower limb discomfort in the setting of an elevated BMI. This information, along with the photograph, which demonstrates haemosiderin staining, suggests chronic venous insufficiency to be the correct answer. The mechanism behind the haemosiderin staining in venous skin changes is due to venous hypertension (due to vein incompetence or occlusion) resulting in increased capillary permeability with resultant tissue oedema, leakage of red cells (breaking down to haemosiderin) and extravasation of activated white cells, leading to an inflammatory cascade. It commonly presents with symptoms such as discomfort, swelling, heaviness, and fatigue in the lower limbs, especially towards the end of the day.

Necrobiosis lipoidica is a rare granulomatous lesion that is most often observed on the shins of diabetic patients. Initially, this condition begins as a dull red papule or plaque on the shin, which slowly enlarges into one or more yellowish-brown patches with a red rim. Whilst this patient does have diabetes, the appearance of the leg as seen in the photograph is not consistent with necrobiosis lipoidica. Here is an example of necrobiosis lipoidica (Image 1).

Erythema nodosum is characterised by tender, erythematous, subcutaneous nodules that typically appear on the anterior surface of the lower legs. These nodules are usually symmetrical, warm, and painful to touch. The condition is often associated with systemic symptoms such as fever, malaise, and joint pain. In this case, the patient's symptoms and the appearance of the skin changes are not consistent with erythema nodosum. The lack of tender, erythematous nodules and the presence of haemosiderin staining make erythema nodosum an unlikely diagnosis. Here is an example of erythema nodosum (Image 2).

Phytodermatitis refers to skin inflammation or irritation caused by contact with plants or plant products. In the case provided, the patient mentions spending considerable time gardening in recent weeks. This history raises the possibility of exposure to various irritants or allergens present in plants, which could lead to a dermatitis reaction. While the patient mentions spending considerable time gardening, there are no specific signs or symptoms suggestive of an acute inflammatory reaction or contact dermatitis described in the case. Therefore, phytodermatitis is less likely based on the provided information.

Pre-tibial myxoedema is less likely in this case due to the absence of characteristic skin changes, such as localised thickening and swelling over the shins with a waxy appearance, which are typically associated with this condition. Additionally, while symptoms such as mild swelling in the lower legs, a feeling of heaviness, occasional fatigue, generalised aches and pains, subtle shifts in mood, and occasional digestive discomfort may suggest underlying thyroid dysfunction, there are no specific signs or symptoms suggestive of thyroid disease described in the case. Furthermore, the patient's medical history primarily includes hypertension, type 2 diabetes mellitus, and gout, with no mention of thyroid-related conditions or symptoms, making pre-tibial myxoedema a less plausible diagnosis. Here is an example of pre-tibial myxoedema (Image 3).
learningPoints: Recognition of chronic venous insufficiency as a cause of lower limb discolouration
references: Mc George S, Walton S. Necrobiosis lipoidica. The British journal of diabetes & vascular disease. 2016;16(1):6–9
Singh A, Zahra F. Chronic Venous Insufficiency. [Updated 2023 Apr 27]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2024 Jan-.
